bonuses.leadin: The speaker of a poem sees this animal “frail, gaunt, and small” when leaning “upon a coppice gate.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this title animal whose song expresses “some blessed Hope, whereof he knew/ and I was unaware.” It is described with the same unusual adjective used for a plain in “Dover Beach.”

In “Pleasure’s Past,” this poet describes a thrush in the Northamptonshire dialect as a “speckt throstle.” This poet narrated a starving, four day escape from a mental asylum in “Journey Out of Essex.”
This poet’s “The Tables Turned” exclaims “how blithe the throstle sings!” This Romantic poet of “Tintern Abbey” collaborated with Coleridge on Lyrical Ballads.
answers: the darkling thrush (The poem is by Thomas Hardy.)
answers_sanitized: the darkling thrush (The poem is by Thomas Hardy.)
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The speaker of a poem sees this animal "frail, gaunt, and small" when leaning "upon a coppice gate." For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this title animal whose song expresses "some blessed Hope, whereof he knew/ and I was unaware." It is described with the same unusual adjective used for a plain in "Dover Beach."

In "Pleasure's Past," this poet describes a thrush in the Northamptonshire dialect as a "speckt throstle." This poet narrated a starving, four day escape from a mental asylum in "Journey Out of Essex."
This poet's "The Tables Turned" exclaims "how blithe the throstle sings!" This Romantic poet of "Tintern Abbey" collaborated with Coleridge on Lyrical Ballads.

bonuses.leadin: In this poem, God says “It will be warmer when I blow the trumpet.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poem delivered from the perspective of a corpse who hears “great guns” that “shook all our coffins as we lay.”
In this other poem written by the author of “Channel Firing,” the speaker leans on a “coppice gate” and notices the title “frail, gaunt, and small” bird.
“Channel Firing” and “The Darkling Thrush” are both poems written by this author of Far from the Madding Crowd and Tess of the D’Urbervilles.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In this poem, God says "It will be warmer when I blow the trumpet." For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this poem delivered from the perspective of a corpse who hears "great guns" that "shook all our coffins as we lay."
In this other poem written by the author of "Channel Firing," the speaker leans on a "coppice gate" and notices the title "frail, gaunt, and small" bird.
"Channel Firing" and "The Darkling Thrush" are both poems written by this author of Far from the Madding Crowd and Tess of the D'Urbervilles.

bonuses.leadin: This poem was first published as “By the Century’s Deathbed” on December 19th, 1900. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poem that proclaims “every spirit upon earth / Seemed fervourless as I.” The narrator of this poem sees “So little cause for carolings” as “Winter’s dregs made desolate / The weakening eye of day.”
This author of “The Darkling Thrush” also wrote The Mayor of Casterbridge and Tess of the d’Urbervilles.

Hardy described one of these events in a poem that notes “The sea-worm crawls” and considers “The Immanent Will that stirs and urges everything.” Gerard Manley Hopkins dedicated a poem about one of these events “To the happy memory of five Franciscan Nuns.”
answers: shipwreck [or equivalents like sinking of a ship; accept “The Wreck of the Deutschland” or sinking of the Titanic]

packet.name: I. Duke A, Georgia Tech B, MSU C, NYU B, Yale A

answers_sanitized: shipwreck [or equivalents like sinking of a ship; accept "The Wreck of the Deutschland" or sinking of the Titanic]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This poem was first published as "By the Century's Deathbed" on December 19th, 1900. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this poem that proclaims "every spirit upon earth / Seemed fervourless as I." The narrator of this poem sees "So little cause for carolings" as "Winter's dregs made desolate / The weakening eye of day."
This author of "The Darkling Thrush" also wrote The Mayor of Casterbridge and Tess of the d'Urbervilles.

Hardy described one of these events in a poem that notes "The sea-worm crawls" and considers "The Immanent Will that stirs and urges everything." Gerard Manley Hopkins dedicated a poem about one of these events "To the happy memory of five Franciscan Nuns."

bonuses.leadin: This poet invoked a metaphor from Jeremiah in the title of a poem declaring that "War's annals will cloud into night / Ere their story die." For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this poet who wrote that "the smile on your mouth was the deadest thing / Alive enough to have strength to die" in his poem "Neutral Tones," penned "In the Time of 'The Breaking of Nations,'" and created a darkly comic depiction of the plight of lower-class women in "The Ruined Maid."

Hardy commemorated the turn of the twentieth century with this poem, his most famous, which describes an aged bird, "frail, gaunt, and small, In blast-beruffled plume," that "had chosen thus to fling his soul / Upon the growing gloom."

Hardy gave this name to the force which he believed shapes the course of human events. In "The Convergence of the Twain," it "stirs and urges everything," including the collision of the Titanic with the iceberg, and it purportedly gains consciousness in Hardy's verse-drama The Dynasts.

Hardy commemorated the turn of the twentieth century with this poem, his most famous, which describes an aged bird, "frail, gaunt, and small, In blast-beruffled plume," that "had chosen thus to fling his soul / Upon the growing gloom."

Hardy gave this name to the force which he believed shapes the course of human events. In "The Convergence of the Twain," it "stirs and urges everything," including the collision of the Titanic with the iceberg, and it purportedly gains consciousness in Hardy's verse-drama The Dynasts.

bonuses.leadin: The sestet of this sonnet laments that "crass Casualty obstructs the sun and rain, and dicing Time for gladness casts a moan." For 10 points each:
parts: Name this sonnet, whose speaker hopes that "some vengeful god would call to me" and laugh, "Thou suffering thing, know that thy sorrow is my ecstasy, that thy love's loss is my hate's profiting!"
"Hap" was written by this poet, who wrote the novels Far From the Madding Crowd and The Mayor of Casterbridge.

In this Hardy poem about the close of the 19th-century, when "every spirit upon earth seemed fervourless as I," he concludes that the title bird sings of "some blessed hope, whereof he knew and I was unaware."
